g.data-narration: File Transfer Protocol (FTP) - A comprehensive guide to understanding file transfer in computer networks.
FTP is a standard network protocol for transferring files between clients and servers on TCP/IP networks.
g.text: File Transfer Protocol (FTP) is a standard
network protocol for transferring files between
a client and server on a computer network.

g.data-narration: FTP provides reliable file transfer with session state, enabling resume, directory navigation, and user authentication.
g.text: • Reliable, ordered data delivery using TCP

g.data-narration: FTP uses two channels: Control Channel on Port 21 for commands, and Data Channel on Port 20 for file transfer.
In Active Mode, the client sends its port number to server. Server then initiates connection back to client.
g.text: Client opens random port → sends PORT command → Server connects from port 20
Server initiates connection → may be blocked

g.data-narration: In Passive Mode, server provides an IP and port. Client initiates both connections, making it firewall-friendly.
g.text: Client sends PASV → Server responds with IP:Port → Client initiates both connections
Client initiates all connections → no blocking

g.data-narration: FTP uses text commands: USER, PASS for authentication; LIST, RETR, STOR for file operations; CWD, PWD for navigation.
FTP servers respond with 3-digit codes: 1xx preliminary, 2xx success, 3xx intermediate, 4xx transient error, 5xx permanent error.
ASCII mode converts line endings for text files. Binary mode transfers exact bytes - use for images, executables, ZIP files.
Anonymous FTP allows public access using username 'anonymous' and email as password. Used for public file distribution.
g.text: Public access without credentials - uses special username:

g.data-narration: FTPS adds SSL/TLS to FTP. SFTP is a different protocol running over SSH - not the same thing!
g.text: Security: FTPS vs SFTP (Not the same!)
AUTH TLS (explicit) or implicit SSL on 990

g.data-narration: A typical FTP session: Connect → Authenticate with USER/PASS → Navigate with CWD → Enter PASV mode → Download with RETR.
Behind firewalls, use passive mode. Active mode fails because server-initiated connections are blocked by corporate firewalls.
g.text: Server can't connect back to client through firewall
Client initiates all connections - firewall allows

g.data-narration: Key exam points: Ports 20/21, active vs passive, ASCII vs binary, response codes, and FTPS vs SFTP difference.
Quick reference: Port 21 for control, Port 20 for data. Use passive mode behind firewalls. Binary mode for non-text files.
